questions.question: What does 'config name' mean when passing issues + config name?

options.label: Named profile in a multi-project config file
options.description: A single ~/.factory/projects.yaml (or similar) with named entries like 'myapp', 'frontend-service' — each entry has its own repo, stages, checks

options.label: Path to a pipeline.yaml for that project
options.description: You pass a path like --config ~/projects/myapp/pipeline.yaml per issue batch, pointing to each project's existing config file

options.label: The pipeline.name field inside each config
options.description: Each config file already has a 'name' field — factory discovers all known configs and you reference them by that name

questions.question: What does 'config name' mean when passing issues + config name?

options.label: Named profile in a multi-project config file
options.description: A single ~/.factory/projects.yaml (or similar) with named entries like 'myapp', 'frontend-service' — each entry has its own repo, stages, checks

options.label: Path to a pipeline.yaml for that project
options.description: You pass a path like --config ~/projects/myapp/pipeline.yaml per issue batch, pointing to each project's existing config file

options.label: The pipeline.name field inside each config
options.description: Each config file already has a 'name' field — factory discovers all known configs and you reference them by that name

answers.What does 'config name' mean when passin: The pipeline.name field inside each config

questions.question: Where do the multiple project configs live, and how does factory discover them?

options.label: Registered in a central index file
options.description: A ~/.factory/projects.yaml (or similar) lists paths to each pipeline.yaml — you register a project once with 'factory project register'

options.label: Scanned from a search path
options.description: Factory scans known directories (e.g. ~/projects/*/pipeline.yaml) to discover configs automatically

options.label: Passed explicitly at call time
options.description: Each invocation specifies config paths inline — e.g. factory queue add --config /path/to/pipeline.yaml 42 43

options.label: Registered in a central index file

options.label: Passed explicitly at call time
options.description: Each invocation specifies config paths inline — e.g. factory queue add --config /path/to/pipeline.yaml 42 43

questions.question: What does the multi-project invocation look like?

options.label: Issues grouped per config flag
options.description: factory queue add --config /path/to/a.yaml 42 43 --config /path/to/b.yaml 101 102

options.label: A batch file (JSON/YAML)
options.description: factory queue add --batch batch.yaml  where the file lists [{config: ..., issues: [42, 43]}, ...]

options.label: Repeated single calls
options.description: Keep 'factory queue add --config ... issue' as today but fix the orchestrator to handle mixed configs in the queue

options.label: Issues grouped per config flag

questions.question: When a pipeline is created for a different project, where does factory find that project's git repo?

options.label: Repo path is encoded in the pipeline config
options.description: Add a 'path' or 'dir' field to pipeline.yaml — factory uses that as the working directory for worktrees and git ops

options.label: Derived from the config file's location
options.description: The pipeline.yaml for project X lives inside the project directory, so its parent dir is the repo root

options.label: Current directory at time of 'pipeline create'
options.description: Whoever calls 'factory pipeline create' must cd into the right repo first — same as today

questions.question: When a pipeline is created for a different project, where does factory find that project's git repo?

options.label: Repo path is encoded in the pipeline config
options.description: Add a 'path' or 'dir' field to pipeline.yaml — factory uses that as the working directory for worktrees and git ops

options.label: Derived from the config file's location
options.description: The pipeline.yaml for project X lives inside the project directory, so its parent dir is the repo root

options.label: Current directory at time of 'pipeline create'
options.description: Whoever calls 'factory pipeline create' must cd into the right repo first — same as today

answers.When a pipeline is created for a differe: Derived from the config file's location

questions.question: Currently all pipeline state lives at ~/.factory/pipelines/{issue}/. If two projects both have issue #42, they'd collide. How should this be namespaced?

options.label: Namespace by config name: ~/.factory/pipelines/{config-name}/{issue}/
options.description: Use the pipeline.name field as a namespace — clean, human-readable, but requires name to be unique across configs

options.label: Namespace by repo: ~/.factory/pipelines/{org}/{repo}/{issue}/
options.description: Derive namespace from the GitHub repo slug in the config — matches GitHub's natural namespace

options.label: Keep flat, rely on config namespacing in queue
options.description: Store the config path in the queue; collision risk is low in practice since most repos have different issue sets

questions.question: Currently all pipeline state lives at ~/.factory/pipelines/{issue}/. If two projects both have issue #42, they'd collide. How should this be namespaced?

options.label: Namespace by config name: ~/.factory/pipelines/{config-name}/{issue}/
options.description: Use the pipeline.name field as a namespace — clean, human-readable, but requires name to be unique across configs

options.label: Namespace by repo: ~/.factory/pipelines/{org}/{repo}/{issue}/
options.description: Derive namespace from the GitHub repo slug in the config — matches GitHub's natural namespace

options.label: Keep flat, rely on config namespacing in queue
options.description: Store the config path in the queue; collision risk is low in practice since most repos have different issue sets

questions.question: How do workers pull work from the centralized input?

options.label: Pull-based: workers poll a central queue and claim work
options.description: Workers ask 'give me the next issue' — like a job queue (SQS, Redis, Postgres-backed). Scales well, workers are interchangeable

options.label: Push-based: central controller assigns issues to specific workers
options.description: A scheduler knows which workers exist and actively routes issues to them — like Celery or a task broker

options.label: Not decided yet — open to recommendation
options.description: You don't have strong opinions on dispatch mechanism, want the design to recommend one

questions.question: How do workers pull work from the centralized input?

options.label: Pull-based: workers poll a central queue and claim work

options.label: Push-based: central controller assigns issues to specific workers
options.description: A scheduler knows which workers exist and actively routes issues to them — like Celery or a task broker

options.label: Not decided yet — open to recommendation
options.description: You don't have strong opinions on dispatch mechanism, want the design to recommend one

answers.How do workers pull work from the centra: Not decided yet — open to recommendation

questions.question: When a worker processes an issue, what does it have access to? (The git repo needs to be somewhere)

options.label: Workers clone/fetch the repo themselves from GitHub
options.description: Each worker pulls a fresh clone or sparse checkout from GitHub. Stateless workers, no shared filesystem needed

options.label: Workers mount a shared NFS / volume with the repo
options.description: A shared filesystem gives all workers access to the same repo. Simpler, but harder to scale across machines

options.label: Not sure — open to recommendation
options.description: Let the design drive this decision based on what makes most sense architecturally

questions.question: When a worker processes an issue, what does it have access to? (The git repo needs to be somewhere)

options.label: Workers clone/fetch the repo themselves from GitHub
options.description: Each worker pulls a fresh clone or sparse checkout from GitHub. Stateless workers, no shared filesystem needed

options.label: Workers mount a shared NFS / volume with the repo
options.description: A shared filesystem gives all workers access to the same repo. Simpler, but harder to scale across machines

options.label: Not sure — open to recommendation
options.description: Let the design drive this decision based on what makes most sense architecturally

answers.When a worker processes an issue, what d: Workers clone/fetch the repo themselves from GitHub

questions.question: If the coordinator is remote, does 'factory' CLI just become a client that talks to it?

options.label: Yes — CLI is a pure client (like kubectl)
options.description: factory queue add, factory pipeline status etc. all become API calls to the remote coordinator. No local state.

options.label: CLI works locally in dev, remote in prod
options.description: factory can run a local coordinator for solo dev, but in team/cloud mode it points to a remote one via a configured endpoint

options.label: Open to recommendation
options.description: Not decided yet — want the design to suggest the right model

questions.question: If the coordinator is remote, does 'factory' CLI just become a client that talks to it?

options.label: Yes — CLI is a pure client (like kubectl)
options.description: factory queue add, factory pipeline status etc. all become API calls to the remote coordinator. No local state.

options.label: CLI works locally in dev, remote in prod

options.label: Open to recommendation
options.description: Not decided yet — want the design to suggest the right model

questions.question: Can there be multiple coordinators running in parallel, or is it always one coordinator managing everything?

options.label: One coordinator, possibly remote (single leader)
options.description: There's always exactly one coordinator. It could live anywhere but it's a singleton. Simpler state management, no consensus needed.

options.label: Multiple coordinators, each owning a subset of projects
options.description: Each coordinator handles certain projects/repos. Sharded by project. No shared state between coordinators.

options.label: Multiple coordinators, all sharing the same state
options.description: True horizontal scaling — any coordinator can handle any work. Requires Postgres + distributed locking. Most complex.

questions.question: Can there be multiple coordinators running in parallel, or is it always one coordinator managing everything?

options.label: Multiple coordinators, all sharing the same state
options.description: True horizontal scaling — any coordinator can handle any work. Requires Postgres + distributed locking. Most complex.

questions.question: What's the primary motivation for going remote? What becomes possible that isn't today?

options.label: Always-on automation
options.description: Factory should run 24/7 processing issues without my laptop being open

options.label: Team access
options.description: Other people need to use factory or see its dashboard

options.label: Reproducible environment
options.description: I want a clean, consistent environment that's easy to rebuild if something breaks

options.label: All of the above
options.description: Some combination of always-on, team access, and reproducibility

questions.question: What's your budget/ops appetite for the remote environment?

options.label: Single VPS
options.description: One Linux box (e.g. Hetzner, DigitalOcean droplet) — I'll SSH in, keep it simple

options.label: Container service
options.description: Something like Fly.io, Railway, or a single-node Docker host — slightly more managed

options.label: Full cloud (AWS/GCP)
options.description: I'm okay with more infra complexity if it buys durability and scalability

options.label: K8s Secret with OAuth token

options.label: API key instead
options.description: I'd prefer switching to direct Anthropic API calls rather than shelling out to the claude CLI

options.description: Not sure what the best approach is yet

options.label: Public, no auth

questions.question: Docker push to DOCR needs a DigitalOcean API token with registry:write scope. Both k8s registry secrets (personalcluster, registry-personalcluster) only have pull access. How should we proceed?

options.label: Provide a DO API token
options.description: You give me a DO personal access token with registry read/write scope. I'll add it to factory-secrets and configure doctl in the container.

options.label: Use doctl from your machine
options.description: Run `doctl registry login` locally, then I'll copy the resulting docker config into the k8s secret with push credentials.

options.label: Skip DOCR, use different approach
options.description: Push from your local machine or CI instead of from the factory pod. The factory deploy agent would skip the push step.
